titleStmt.title: Saudi Arabia 2022 – Speaker 20 – Traditional Tools
WIBARAB Corpus
titleStmt.funder: Funded by the European Research Council under the Grant Agreement 101020127

publicationStmt.distributor: Austrian Center for Digital Humanities and Cultural Heritage

recording.p: Recorded during Saudi Arabia Fieldwork Campaign Gunda Kinzl 2022

prefixDef.p: Private URIs using the teiCorpusHeader prefix are pointers to any element in the WIBARAB teiCorpus document.
Private URIs using the share prefix are pointers to audio file residing on the WIBARAB network share.
Private URIs using the geo prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a place element in VICAV Geodata list.
Private URIs using the dmp prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB DMP document..
Private URIs using the zotid prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on a biblStruct element in the TEI export of the VICAV Zotero Group library.
Private URIs using the sources prefix are pointers to the xml:id attribute on an element in the WIBARAB list of sources.

spanGrp.span: a basket, one puts the dates in it
This is how it was before
No,no,no. Well, this is the basket
The basket, one puts the dates in it
This is a basket with the shape of a plate
now, import, this was the plate of previous times, where is the plate?
and this plate like this one, the people put (things) on it, this is the small one and this is the big one
this is the cloth (that one spreads on the floor to eat)
this is the cloth, for the food on the tablet (ṣīnīya - also tablet)
produced from..yes, the plate, yes
a big plate, (for) invitations/events
they use it
This is the water pouch
until now, in travelling, no, there are people, (by God), they still use it
it is almost obsolete
The older people
an old person, someone up there in age for example
someone who is 80 years, 85 years and over, God bless them, there are still people that use it even nowadays,in events / invitations, when they have an invite
they love it, it is old from their generation
(passive) it is produced from the palm leaves
this is much better
(from the) root, plastic is chemical but this is from the palm trees
all of this is produced the palm trees
everything is from the palm trees
the palm trees are the foundation of everything, everything comes from the palm trees.
I
Now we leave from here and we look for the palm trees in front of us in nature, everything is produced... (trochaism giddām-a-na)
The (f.) bedouins
they (f.pl.) take it from the sedentary women
they find it at the sed. woman who uses it
this is the water pouch
for the water
(for the) water, for the yoghurt, for the-, they milk the sheep and there is milk of course
this, this is filled with water
this, he rides the camel
they ride the camel
they go to the well
here
this is the well
they go to the well
the well, the well
(a chant which they do probably when they are going to the well or when they are collecting the water)
they pour (it) in the water pouch
they pour water in it, yes, they pour it
they extract (or lit. they raise up from it) all the fat and the butter
# and fat
naturally, in the beginning (it is) the milk, they milk the sheep, afterwards they like to, they milk the sheep and they pour it in the water pouch, the same system
they impregnate the camel
and they shake, they shake and they do like this for a long time, they shake until yoghurt emerges from the milk
they shake it possibly for half an hour or 20 minutes, for a long time and then it becomes yoghurt, ready to be drunk and they extract butter from it (the min-a refers to ḥalīb)
butter emerges from it, and from the butter then they take the fat(the /ʕ/ in biʕdēn is very very soft)
yes, the butter is transformed into fat
This is a basket, they make the basket, this is a basket for the dates, for the stuff of the basket, before there wasn't, there wasn't
This is the same system, this is the way, this is the basket
there isn't, yes, there isn't
yes, there isn't, this is a big basket and there is a small basket
In Fayfa we call it zinbīl

We call it zibīl, it does not concern our dialect, we call it zibīl, it is not our business what they call it in the dialects of the south, this is the basket, and this, meaning, one puts dates, one puts, it is a smaller basket (mā-lna - not concerning our dialect, mā-_lna šuġul - not our business, maʕlaf - a smaller basket (to scoop with))
this is the maʕlaf (a smaller basket), for every size there is a name
this is a small basket, one puts a few dates in it, they take it, abu-firas and # Abu Marzūg and all of it from the dates
this small one, they call it kāšūla, yes, kāšūla, and that which is bigger than it is called a zibīl like these big ones, the small one is called kāšūla, yes
kāšūla, kāšūla
I say, I say zibīl but abū firās he says a small (dim.) kāšūla
the small one is called a kāšūla, this is the big basket, the big ones here (presentative)
this is the basket, one puts a lot of dates and store (them) in it
and the smaller basket (kāšūla) one would take it and give it to the family and they (the family) would eat from it
or he would take it and eat from it because it is small (lan - liʔanna)
yes, this is true, yes
look here, look, how is this?
look, the cover, the zibīl, the cover is put on it like in the modernly produced ones ((but it is maḥṭūṭ) the ḥ is replaced by a ʕ, also theʔ is dropped from ġaṭāʔ)
yes, correct, look, the palm trees, they make everthing from them, they produce everything from the palm tree, good grace, everything, (from) the leaves of the palm trees
these, these are called the leaves, of the palm trees (ysamma - passive)
these are the leaves, we have leaves outside, these are the leaves, the leaves of the palm trees, this is them, these are the leaves, these, these are the leaves, these come from the palm trees
the leaf, this is from the palm trees
all products of it
Everything is produced (from it), these are from, these are made by the women, they produce from it a broom
This is a broom for cleaning, this is a broom, there are people who still use it to this day, they still use it, it is nice (why li-ḥadda and not li ḥatta)
here is a broomstick
yes, a broomstick like, there isn't (in it)
the cleaning, they use it, we don't have electric broomsticks and we have, not a lot, thank God

but there are people who are used to it, especially the elderly, for example, our uncle, let's say those 50 and over and those energetic 50 year old, thank God, and those who are in their sixties and up to their seventies
for cleaning
their (f.pl.) moment, their moment is over ab firās, now their time is over
they fix from it, they fix with it
here, here is a broomstick
a fan, there isn't, a broomstick
we call it a broom, or a fan, same thing
a fan, a fan, cold
everthing from this, this one is made, this fan is excellent
here, here is a spindle
(to be checked whether its summer or a weird form of ṣūf)
a bedouin woman makes a house from hair
here is a fan (minfāša another word for fan) made from the wool, the wool
one puts it here and she holds it together and it twists in this shape
and it twists and twists and it becomes string
a broom
for the cleaning of the place and in the room
this is a fan, a bedouin woman would grab the wool, the wool
the sheep, you understand? (he says a very soft m at the end of maʕlūm, almost maʕlū)
this, she takes it and puts it her and she shapes it and brushes it(tigūl-ha - here means to make)
it becomes soft and then we bring this spindle
it makes the string, the string
explain this to us
this is, this is a spindle
this is, this is a #
a moment, abū firās?
this is a nice thing this, you are benefitting from these words?
this is what you want
abū firās? a trap, for the birds
this is a trap, a trap for the birds and then... it catches
it catches the bird yes
the birds, she is recording, don't say birds, the birds
the birds, ok, the birds
It captures birds, it captures the rat, the rats (fahar - fiʔr mouse)
no, these are all for the birds, yes for the bird, for the birds, a bird which flies, the birds
these are for the birds, for the birds
for the hawks
yes, the birds, yes this would capture the birds
this is, explain it, and this is from the Hijaz
Did you see? this is a trap which captures the birds, the trap
the birds come and one would things here and it would lean into it
one would put seeds and afterwards a bird would come and it springs and afterwards it locks, and captures
it locks
no, no, it remains alive, it remains alive and it is captures for example
yes, it remains alive, yes
all of it or

here they take the string and at the end of the string they put a base (markaz) and then they put a barrier that is light, or they put another string that cannot be seen that is lighter then the birds come and move it
(ḥaǧal - partridge)
this, this is for, he puts a basin from, from, from leather
from leather, and he puts here and he puts here
to purify the water
mirkāba - something that you put sth on
-ila - ʕala
there werent plates there werent
what is it called, what are they called
the base of a basin
yissaffat - folded like this
and he puts it, when we arrive to the well
dalu - bucket, albil -pl
brīg kettle
and he puts it over this
it's the same one we brought it from they and we put it her
needs work
mungul is a type of grill
